title: Compact graph component spacing
description: Why this exists: visual inspection shows the force-directed graph still places graph parts too far apart. What needs to be done: tune force layout, camera framing, and possibly add explicit compactness behavior so connected components and clusters sit closer together.
close_reason: Reduced repulsion, tightened link distances, added compacting center force, and visually verified real graph desktop/mobile clustering.

title: Add explicit graph zoom controls
description: Why this exists: the 3D graph supports wheel/trackpad zoom, but the UI does not show visible zoom controls. What needs to be done: add explicit zoom in and zoom out controls to the graph toolbar and wire them to the 3D camera.
close_reason: Added visible zoom in/out toolbar controls wired to the 3D graph camera, with tests and browser smoke verification.

title: Tighten clustered graph styling
description: Why this exists: the force-directed 3D graph currently renders nodes with planet-like rings and the layout spreads nodes too far apart. What needs to be done: remove ring/planet styling, use simpler node markers, and tune force/layout/camera parameters so clusters are more compact.
close_reason: Removed planet ring styling, reduced node and arrow sizes, tightened force layout distances, and replaced zoom-to-fit with stable compact camera framing.

title: Use clustered force-directed 3D graph layout
description: Why this exists: the current 3D graph uses a deterministic sphere/depth layout, but the user wants a PuppyGraph-like clustered force-directed graph with better natural grouping. What needs to be done: replace or augment the custom Three.js layout with a lightweight force-directed 3D graph tool, preserve existing controls and interactions, and verify rendering.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The graph view uses a force-directed clustered 3D layout rather than the previous deterministic sphere/depth layout. 2. It visually resembles the provided PuppyGraph-style reference more closely, with connected nodes naturally clustering. 3. Existing matter status colors, selected state, dependency arrows, labels, filters, inspector, editing controls, and command panel continue to work. 4. Clicking a node selects it and fades unrelated nodes/links into the background. 5. Node dragging does not reposition an individual matter as saved state; grabbing a node should still be usable to rotate/orbit the graph. 6. The UI still runs through matters web --state. 7. WebGL fallback remains clear. 8. Existing tests pass and frontend asset tests cover the force-directed graph dependency.
close_reason: Switched the 3D graph surface to 3d-force-graph with clustered force-directed layout, labels, arrows, filtering, focus fading, WebGL fallback, docs, and tests.

title: Make matters graph view 3D
description: Why this exists: the current browser UI renders the matters graph as a 2D SVG. The user wants the graph view to become 3D while preserving graph operations, editing, filtering, and the command panel. What needs to be done: design and implement a 3D graph visualization for the web UI with camera controls, selection/focus behavior, status styling, and practical fallback/testing.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The main graph view renders matters as 3D nodes and dependencies as 3D directed edges. 2. Users can rotate, pan, and zoom the camera with mouse/trackpad controls. 3. Users can grab a node and drag to rotate the whole graph around all axes, using the node as a handle rather than moving only that node. 4. Node colors/statuses remain clear for resolved, actionable/universe, blocked, and selected matters. 5. Clicking a node selects it and updates the existing inspector. 6. When a node is selected, unrelated nodes and edges fade into the visual background, while directly connected nodes/edges remain prominent. 7. Text search and status filters continue to affect the graph. 8. Existing create matter, condition editing, dependency editing, and command/chat panel workflows continue to work. 9. Graph-view buttons for universe, frontier, horizon, and unlock still work. 10. The 3D view has a reset-camera control. 11. If WebGL is unavailable, the UI shows a clear fallback message instead of a blank graph. 12. The implementation remains easy to run through matters web --state. 13. Existing tests continue to pass, with added tests where practical.
notes: Software-factory checkpoint: user approved revised 3D graph story on 2026-06-01. Key additional interaction: grabbing a node and dragging rotates the whole graph around all axes; it must not drag only the individual node.
close_reason: Implemented Three.js 3D graph view with WebGL fallback, node-handle graph rotation across axes, camera controls, status styling, focus fading, docs, and tests.

title: Build browser-based matters graph UI
description: Why this exists: matters.global currently exposes graph operations through CLI/text outputs and has only a decorative docs canvas, not an actual browser UI for interacting with saved matters graphs. What needs to be done: design and implement a web-based GUI that renders the real matters graph with nodes and edges, supports zooming/filtering, lets users create matters/conditions/dependencies, and provides a chat/control panel for agent-assisted operations such as universe, frontier, horizon, unlock, extraction, and other matters workflows.
design: Approved story adjustments: node selection must fade non-connected graph elements into the visual background; common operations must be available in both the chat/control panel and graph interface; v1 should be a local web server included in the Python package, e.g. matters web.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The app launches locally via a packaged command such as matters web and opens in a browser. 2. The main view renders the real matters JSON state as graph nodes and directed dependency edges. 3. The graph supports pan and zoom. 4. Nodes visually distinguish resolved, actionable/universe, blocked, and selected states. 5. The UI filters by status and text search. 6. Selecting a node shows id, conditions, prerequisites, dependents, derived status, and fades non-connected nodes/edges into the visual background. 7. Users can create a matter with one or more conditions. 8. Users can add, edit, and toggle condition truth values. 9. Users can create and remove dependencies between existing matters. 10. Universe, frontier, horizon, unlock, and basic create/extract-style operations are available from both the chat/control panel and graph view controls. 11. V1 chat may be a local command interface backed by the matters engine; direct Codex/Claude adapter integration can follow once a stable local agent API is available. 12. Changes persist to the same JSON state format used by the CLI. 13. Existing CLI and tests continue to work. 14. Docs explain how to run the web UI.
notes: Software-factory checkpoint: user approved updated story on 2026-06-01. Key approved requirements: local browser UI via matters web; real graph rendering; pan/zoom/filter; node selection inspector; fade non-connected nodes/edges on selection; create matters/conditions/dependencies; chat/control panel plus graph-view controls for universe/frontier/horizon/unlock/basic create/extract operations; persist to existing JSON state; keep CLI/tests working.
close_reason: Implemented matters web local graph UI with API, SVG graph, filters, editing controls, command panel, docs, and tests.

title: Remove Public demo hero badge
description: Remove the 'Public demo' hero status badge from the matters.global public site hero section.
acceptance_criteria: The hero no longer renders the Public demo/next visible step status badge; layout remains valid.
notes: Removed the hero status badge containing 'Public demo' and 'next visible step' from docs/index.html. Removed the now-unused .hero-status CSS rules and responsive overrides from docs/assets/styles.css. Verification: static smoke check confirmed those strings/classes are gone; pytest passed with 25 tests.
close_reason: Removed Public demo hero badge

title: Update personal matters graph after website work
description: Update /Users/matthias/.local/share/matters/matters.json to reflect completed matters.global website and quick-start work from this session.
acceptance_criteria: Relevant conditions in the explicit matters state file are updated based on verified completed work, without changing unrelated matters.
notes: Updated /Users/matthias/.local/share/matters/matters.json based on verified public matters.global site work. Marked true: publish_matters_global_system / First public demo or walkthrough is published; develop_matters_global_public_story / Core narrative is articulated; develop_matters_global_public_story / At least one short public version of the story is published. Left false: People outside the project have been invited to try it, because no invitations were verified. Validation: json.tool passed; matters universe and unlock loaded the state successfully.
close_reason: Updated explicit matters state with verified website/story progress

title: Replace technical Quick Start state note
description: Replace the technical local-state note in the matters.global Quick Start with user-facing copy about starting messy and letting the agent propose matters.
acceptance_criteria: Quick Start no longer mentions the default local state path in the note; it uses the approved 'Start messy...' wording.
notes: Replaced the technical default-state note in docs/index.html with the approved user-facing copy: 'Start messy. Tell the agent what you are trying to understand or move forward...' Verification: HTML smoke check passed and pytest passed with 25 tests.
close_reason: Quick Start note replaced with approved user-facing copy

title: Revise Quick Start for agent-first workflow
description: Update matters.global Quick Start so it tells users to install matters.global, start an agent such as Codex or Claude, and use the system through the agent workflow instead of presenting the CLI as the primary path.
acceptance_criteria: Quick Start on the public site clearly presents the AI/agent route first, with install, agent start, and natural-language use examples; CLI commands remain available only as supporting context if useful.
notes: Revised docs/index.html Quick Start from CLI-first to agent-first. It now tells users to install matters.global, start Codex or Claude in a workspace, and use natural-language prompts to create/review matters, inspect actionable work, and extract candidates while asking before saving. CLI commands remain as supporting context for direct inspection. Verification: HTML parser smoke check passed, local HTTP server served revised copy, pytest passed with 25 tests.
close_reason: Quick Start revised for agent-first workflow

title: Add Quick Start guide to matters.global
description: Explain on matters.global how a new person can get started with the Matters tool.
acceptance_criteria: The public site includes a visible Quick Start section or page with install/setup, first state file, creating matters, inspecting universe, and next-step commands.
notes: Added a visible Quick Start section to docs/index.html and linked it from the hero and primary navigation. The section covers install, resolved state path, creating a first dependency chain, universe/unlock, explicit state files, and extraction from notes. Added responsive CSS for command blocks and quick-start cards. Verification: local HTTP server returned index and stylesheet successfully; HTML parser smoke check found required quick-start commands; pytest passed with 25 tests.
close_reason: Quick Start section added to public site

title: Improve extraction skill for creativity research graph building
description: Improve the extraction skill or underlying matters CLI so creativity research sources yield useful candidate matters, observable conditions, and dependency proposals.
acceptance_criteria: Extraction results on the test corpus are materially better against the documented expectations, and automated or reproducible checks cover the changed behavior.
notes: Improved extractor behavior for creativity research corpus. Added speaker-prefixed marker parsing for conversation lines such as 'Agent: Goal: ...'. Added creativity/research-specific condition labels covering source context, evidence quality/limits, review/persistence, research gaps, transfer conditions, and graph impact. Reduced dependency proposal noise by ignoring generic overlap words before proposing name-overlap dependencies. Added regression tests for speaker-prefixed markers, domain-specific conditions, and dependency-noise reduction. Verification: uv run --with pytest --with-editable . pytest => 25 passed. Manual corpus check: conversation fixture now extracts 4 marker candidates instead of falling back to the first unstructured line.
close_reason: Improved extraction behavior and covered it with tests

title: Test current extraction skill on creativity research sources
description: Run the current extraction skill/CLI against the creativity research corpus and record where candidate matters, conditions, deduplication, or dependency proposals are weak.
acceptance_criteria: Test output is captured; specific failure modes and improvement targets are recorded as notes or follow-up Beads.
notes: Baseline run completed against examples/creativity_research/ using /Users/matthias/.local/share/matters/matters.json. research_notes extracted 6 candidates but with generic placeholder conditions and many noisy name-overlap dependencies. conversation extracted only the first unstructured line and missed speaker-prefixed embedded markers like 'Agent: Goal: ...'. paper_excerpt extracted 5 candidates but also produced generic conditions and noisy dependencies. Current tests still pass: uv run --with pytest --with-editable . pytest => 22 passed. Improvement targets: parse speaker-prefixed markers, produce domain-aware condition labels for creativity/research matters, reduce dependency noise by ignoring generic overlap words and scoring meaningful terms, and align IDs with expected.json where possible.
close_reason: Baseline extraction behavior captured against creativity research corpus

title: Define creativity research extraction test corpus
description: Select representative source material for creativity research extraction: notes, papers, conversations, URLs or markdown exports.
acceptance_criteria: A small test corpus exists or is referenced, with expected high-level matters, condition quality expectations, and dependency-review expectations.
notes: Defined creativity research extraction corpus under examples/creativity_research/: research_notes.txt, conversation.txt, paper_excerpt.txt, and expected.json. The expected file records high-level target matters, condition quality expectations, dependency-review expectations, and known baseline gaps. JSON fixture validates with jq.
close_reason: Creativity research extraction corpus and expected outcomes added

title: Harden extraction skill for creativity research
description: Test and improve the matters extraction workflow enough to start building a useful matters graph for creativity research.
acceptance_criteria: The extraction skill has representative creativity-research tests; identified extraction gaps are fixed or tracked; the workflow can produce reviewed matters and dependency candidates for a creativity-research graph.
notes: Extraction track completed through a reviewable graph proposal. Added corpus fixtures, captured baseline gaps, improved extraction behavior, added tests, and exported examples/creativity_research/initial_graph_proposal.json for explicit acceptance before live-state persistence.
close_reason: Extraction workflow hardened and initial creativity graph proposal exported

title: Verify matters.global website delivery
description: Verify end-to-end that matters.global serves the intended website over HTTPS after DNS propagation.
acceptance_criteria: matters.global loads the intended site over HTTPS; certificate is valid; redirect/canonical behavior for www vs apex is recorded.
notes:
Verification after DNS fix: authoritative UDAG and public resolvers return the corrected records. HTTP apex serves GitHub Pages. HTTPS apex reaches GitHub Pages but certificate is not yet valid for matters.global. GitHub Pages API reports https_enforced=false; attempting PUT /repos/matthiasroder/matters.global/pages with https_enforced=true returned 'The certificate does not exist yet' (HTTP 404). Local resolver still had no www response at check time, while public resolvers had the correct CNAME. Next step: wait for DNS/cache/certificate provisioning, then rerun verification and enable HTTPS enforcement.
Retry to enable HTTPS on 2026-05-31: GitHub Pages API still reports https_certificate=null and https_enforced=false. PUT /repos/matthiasroder/matters.global/pages with https_enforced=true returned 'The certificate does not exist yet' (HTTP 404). Public resolvers 1.1.1.1, 8.8.8.8, and 9.9.9.9 resolve www.matters.global CNAME matthiasroder.github.io and GitHub Pages A records. HTTPS cannot be enforced until GitHub creates the certificate.

title: Fix matters.global DNS for GitHub Pages
description: Apply the DNS and GitHub Pages configuration changes needed for matters.global to serve the website.
acceptance_criteria: Required A/AAAA/CNAME records and GitHub Pages custom-domain configuration are corrected, with no conflicting records left in place.
notes:
Requires DNS provider access for matters.global, apparently served by United Domains nameservers ns.udag.net/ns.udag.org/ns.udag.de. Apply these DNS changes: keep apex A records 185.199.108.153, 185.199.109.153, 185.199.110.153, 185.199.111.153; replace the stale apex AAAA 2001:1a50:11:0:9c77:dff:fea4:2e8d with GitHub Pages AAAA records 2606:50c0:8000::153, 2606:50c0:8001::153, 2606:50c0:8002::153, 2606:50c0:8003::153; add CNAME www.matters.global -> matthiasroder.github.io. After propagation, GitHub Pages HTTPS can be enabled.
Verified after user update. Authoritative UDAG nameservers now return correct apex A records, correct GitHub Pages apex AAAA records, and www.matters.global CNAME matthiasroder.github.io. Public resolvers 1.1.1.1, 8.8.8.8, and 9.9.9.9 also return the new www CNAME and GitHub AAAA records. Local resolver still had stale/no www response at verification time, consistent with resolver cache propagation.
close_reason: DNS records corrected and verified on authoritative nameservers and public resolvers

title: Audit matters.global GitHub Pages and DNS configuration
description: Inspect the current GitHub Pages settings, repository Pages configuration, CNAME file if any, and DNS records for matters.global.
acceptance_criteria: Current repo Pages target, custom-domain setting, CNAME state, registrar/DNS records, and observed DNS/HTTP behavior are documented in the issue notes.
notes: Audit completed 2026-05-31. Repo is public matthiasroder/matters.global. GitHub Pages API reports status=built, custom domain cname=matters.global, source=main:/docs, build_type=legacy, public=true, https_enforced=false. Local Pages source has docs/CNAME containing exactly 'matters.global' and docs/index.html exists. DNS authority is ns.udag.net/ns.udag.org/ns.udag.de. Apex A records are correct for GitHub Pages: 185.199.108.153, 185.199.109.153, 185.199.110.153, 185.199.111.153. Apex AAAA is wrong/stale: 2001:1a50:11:0:9c77:dff:fea4:2e8d, which serves the old Uberspace/nginx WordPress site. www.matters.global has no A, AAAA, or CNAME records. HTTP behavior confirms split routing: curl -4 http://matters.global returns GitHub Pages; curl -6 http/https returns Uberspace; curl -4 https://matters.global reaches GitHub Pages but certificate is not valid yet; www does not resolve. Required DNS fix: replace/remove stale apex AAAA and use GitHub Pages AAAA records 2606:50c0:8000::153, 2606:50c0:8001::153, 2606:50c0:8002::153, 2606:50c0:8003::153; add www CNAME to matthiasroder.github.io; after propagation enable/enforce HTTPS in GitHub Pages.
close_reason: Audit documented repo Pages config, DNS records, and live IPv4/IPv6 HTTP behavior

title: Publish matters.global via GitHub Pages
description: Make the custom domain matters.global reliably point to the project's GitHub Pages website.
acceptance_criteria: matters.global resolves to the intended GitHub Pages site; HTTPS is valid; apex/www behavior is explicit and tested.
